Data Science i AI w bankowości - lista 2 część 2 - Dobór Modelu XGBoost¶

Zuzanna Nogala

In [1]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick

import shap

import json

import xgboost as xgb
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, cross_val_score, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score, roc_curve, make_scorer
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance

from skopt.space import Integer, Real
from skopt import BayesSearchCV
In [2]:
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", context="notebook", palette="viridis", font_scale=0.9)
sns.set_style({'grid.linestyle': '--'})
plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (10, 6)

Funkcje¶

In [19]:
def gini(true_val, predicted_val):
    return 2 * roc_auc_score(true_val, predicted_val) - 1

def model_summary(model, model_name, model_description, 
                  train_set, test_set, valid_set, 
                  y_train, y_test, y_valid):
    
    results_df = pd.DataFrame({'Model': [model_name],
                              'Model_Description': [model_description],
                              'Number_of_Vars': [model.n_features_in_],
                              'Gini_Train': [gini(y_train, model.predict_proba(train_set)[:, 1])],
                              'Gini_Test': [gini(y_test, model.predict_proba(test_set)[:, 1])],
                              'Gini_Valid': [gini(y_valid, model.predict_proba(valid_set)[:, 1])]})
    
    return results_df

def fi_plot(columns_name, metric_df, metric_name, ax=None, title='Feature Importance'):
    metric_df_to_plot = metric_df.loc[metric_df.variable.isin(columns_name)]

    sns.barplot(data = metric_df_to_plot,
            x = f'{metric_name}_norm',
            y = 'variable',
            ax=ax)
    plt.plot()
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel(f'{metric_name} metric')

def get_feats_plots(model, train_set, y_train, n_repeats=10, random_state=0):
    metric_name = 'gain'
    metric_dict = model.get_booster().get_score(importance_type = metric_name)

    metric_df = pd.DataFrame({'variable': metric_dict.keys(), metric_name: metric_dict.values()})
    metric_df[f'{metric_name}_norm'] = metric_df[metric_name] / metric_df[metric_name].sum()
    metric_df = metric_df.sort_values(f'{metric_name}_norm', ascending=False)

    pi_results = permutation_importance(model, train_set, y_train, n_repeats=n_repeats, random_state=random_state)

    pi_df = pd.DataFrame({'variable': train_set.columns, 
                      'importance_norm': pi_results['importances_mean']}).sort_values('importance_norm', ascending=False)

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(18, 8))
    #axes = axes.flatten()

    fi_plot(columns_name = train_set.columns, metric_df = metric_df, metric_name = metric_name, title='Feature Importance - GAIN metric', ax=axes[0])
    fi_plot(columns_name = train_set.columns, metric_df = pi_df, metric_name = "importance", title='Feature Importance - permutation', ax=axes[1])
    plt.show()

def draw_gini_vs_variable(model, train_set, y_train, test_set, y_test, title_name, params=None, n_repeats=10, random_state=42):
    pi_results = permutation_importance(model, train_set, y_train, n_repeats=n_repeats, random_state=random_state)

    pi_df = pd.DataFrame({'variable': train_set.columns, 
                        'importance': pi_results['importances_mean']}
                        ).sort_values('importance', 
                                      ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)
    
    results_df = pi_df.copy()
    results_df['gini_train'] = 0.0
    results_df['gini_test'] = 0.0
    
    for i in range(pi_df.shape[0]):
        vars = pi_df.iloc[0:(i + 1), 0]
        subset_train_set = train_set.loc[:, train_set.columns.isin(vars)]
        subset_test_set = test_set.loc[:, test_set.columns.isin(vars)]

        if params is None:
            model = xgb.XGBClassifier(**params)
        else:
            model = xgb.XGBClassifier(**params)

        model.fit(subset_train_set, y_train)
        train_preds = model.predict_proba(subset_train_set)[:, 1]
        test_preds = model.predict_proba(subset_test_set)[:, 1]

        results_df.loc[i, 'gini_train'] = gini(y_train, train_preds)
        results_df.loc[i, 'gini_test'] = gini(y_test, test_preds)
    
    
    plt.plot(results_df.gini_train, label = 'Gini w zbiorze treningowym', marker='o')
    plt.plot(results_df.gini_test, label = 'Gini w zbiorze testowym', marker='o')
    plt.legend()
    plt.xlabel('Wazność zmiennej')
    plt.ylabel('Wartość Giniego')
    plt.title(title_name)
    plt.show()

    return results_df

def roc_curve_plot(y_true, y_pred, dataset_type = 'TRAIN'):
    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_true, y_pred)

    auc = roc_auc_score(y_true, y_pred)
    sns.lineplot(x = fpr, y = tpr, label = f"{dataset_type} - AUC = {np.round(auc, 3)}")
    # sns.lineplot(x = np.arange(0,1.1,0.1), y = np.arange(0,1.1,0.1), color = 'black')

    ax = plt.gca()
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.PercentFormatter(xmax=1, decimals=0))
    ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.PercentFormatter(xmax=1, decimals=0))

    plt.xlabel("TPR")
    plt.ylabel("FPR")

Wstęp¶

Celem raportu jest budowa i optymalizacja modelu klasyfikacyjnego opartego na algorytmie XGBoost. Model posłuży do prognozowania skłonności klientów do założenia lokaty terminowej w portugalskiej instytucji bankowej na podstawie ich profilu demograficznego, statusu finansowego oraz historii dotychczasowych kontaktów marketingowych. Dane obejmują interakcje realizowane od maja 2008 roku do listopada 2010 roku.

Szczegółowa analiza jakościowa oraz eksploracyjna tego zbioru danych została szczegółowo przedstawiona w części 1 raportu, tj. "Data Science i AI w bankowości - lista 2 część 1 - Jakość Danych".

Raport obejmuje następujące kroki badawcze:

  • analizę i ocenę podstawowych wariantów modelu XGBoost: budowę modeli bazowych na pełnym zestawie zmiennych objaśniających oraz ocenę ich zdolności dyskryminacyjnej przy użyciu współczynnika Giniego ($Gini = 2 \cdot AUC - 1$).
  • selekcję zmiennych i optymalizację struktury: wybór najbardziej efektywnego zestawu predyktorów w celu eliminacji współliniowości zidentyfikowanej w poprzedniej części analizy.
  • wybór modelu wiodącego (Champion): wyłonienie najlepszej konfiguracji algorytmu na podstawie metryk jakości na wydzielonych zbiorach.
  • szczegółową analitykę modelu Champion: interpretację działania wybranego modelu w oparciu o ranking ważności cech (Feature Importance) oraz ocenę stabilności predykcji i dopasowania do danych.
In [4]:
# Dane bazowe
df = pd.read_csv("bank-full.csv", sep = ';')
df['client_id'] = df.index
df['y'] = df['y'].map({'no': 0, 'yes': 1})

# Dane, gdzie zmienne kategoryczne zostały przekształcone na numerycznie (Label Encoding)
df_labeled = df.copy()

encoder = LabelEncoder()
for col in df_labeled.select_dtypes(include=['object', 'string']).columns:
    df_labeled[col] = encoder.fit_transform(df_labeled[col])

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(df_labeled.drop(columns = ['y', 'client_id']), 
                                                    df_labeled.y, test_size=0.4, random_state=20)
X_test, X_valid, y_test, y_valid = train_test_split(X_test, 
                                                    y_test, test_size=0.5, random_state=20)

# Dane, gdzie zmienne kategoryczne zostały przekształcone na zmienne typu Dummy (One-Hot/Dummy Encoding)
df_to_dummys = pd.read_csv("bank-full.csv", sep = ';')
df_to_dummys['y'] = df_to_dummys['y'].map({'no': 0, 'yes': 1})

df_dummy = pd.get_dummies(df_to_dummys[[i for i in df_to_dummys.columns if i!= 'y']])

df_dummy = df_dummy.drop(columns = ['job_admin.', 'marital_divorced', 'education_primary', 'default_no', 
                         'housing_no', 'loan_no', 'contact_cellular', 'month_apr', 'poutcome_failure'])

X_train_d = df_dummy.iloc[X_train.index, :].copy()
X_test_d = df_dummy.iloc[X_test.index, :].copy()
X_valid_d = df_dummy.iloc[X_valid.index, :].copy()

1. Analiza podstawowych wariantów modeli XGBoost¶

W tej sekcji przeprowadzona zostanie analiza i ocena efektywności bazowych konfiguracji klasyfikatora XGBoost. Głównym celem tego etapu jest wyznaczenie punktu odniesienia (baseline) dla dalszych etapów optymalizacji hiperparametrów oraz zweryfikowanie, jak model XGBoost reaguje na odmienne sposoby reprezentacji danych. Zweryfikujemy, czy algorytm osiągnie wyższą zdolność dyskryminacyjną na zmiennych kategorycznych zakodowanych numerycznie (Label Encoding), czy też optymalniejszym wyborem okaże się przekształcenie ich w binarne zmienne fikcyjne (One-Hot / Dummy Encoding).

1.1 Modele z domyślnymi parametrami¶

In [ ]:
model1 = xgb.XGBClassifier()
model1.fit(X_train, y_train)
In [6]:
get_feats_plots(model1, X_train, y_train)
In [7]:
get_feats_plots(model1, X_test, y_test)
In [5]:
model1_results = model_summary(model1, 'Model 1', 'Model z domyślnymi parametrami i z zastosowaniem Label Encoding',
                                X_train, X_test, X_valid, 
                                y_train, y_test, y_valid)

model1_results.style.format('{:.2%}', subset = ['Gini_Train', 'Gini_Test', 'Gini_Valid'])
Out[5]:
  Model Model_Description Number_of_Vars Gini_Train Gini_Test Gini_Valid
0 Model 1 Model z domyślnymi parametrami i z zastosowaniem Label Encoding 16 97.05% 87.49% 86.22%

Model oparty na pełnym zestawie zmiennych kategorycznych zakodowanych numerycznie przy domyślnych ustawieniach hiperparametrów, wykazuje wyraźne symptomy przeuczenia (overfittingu). Świadczy o tym wysoka wartość współczynnika Gini na zbiorze treningowym przy jednoczesnych mniejszych wynikach tej metryki na zbiorach testowym i walidacyjnym.

Zjawisko to potwierdza analiza ważności cech. Rankingi predyktorów wyznaczone dla zbioru treningowego na podstawie zysku (Gain) oraz ważności permutacyjnej (Permutation Importance) wykazują istotne rozbieżności z metrykami wyznaczonymi na zbiorach testowych. Co kluczowe, ewaluacja ważności permutacyjnej bezpośrednio na danych testowych ujawniła, że część zmiennych generuje szum – ich obecność w modelu wręcz obniża jego globalną moc dyskryminacyjną (np. zmienna balance ma ujemną ważność permutacyjną).

W kolejnym kroku analogicznej ocenie poddano model oparty na domyślnych parametrach, lecz wytrenowany na przestrzeni cech przekształconych do postaci Dummy.

In [ ]:
model2 = xgb.XGBClassifier()
model2.fit(X_train_d, y_train)
In [9]:
get_feats_plots(model2, X_train_d, y_train)
In [10]:
get_feats_plots(model2, X_test_d, y_test)
In [ ]:
model2_results =  model_summary(model2, 'Model 2', 'Model z domyślnymi parametrami z zastosowaniem Dummy Encoding',
                                X_train_d, X_test_d, X_valid_d, 
                                y_train, y_test, y_valid)

models_results = pd.concat([model1_results, model2_results]).reset_index(drop = True)
models_results.style.format('{:.2%}', subset = ['Gini_Train', 'Gini_Test', 'Gini_Valid'])
Out[ ]:
  Model Model_Description Number_of_Vars Gini_Train Gini_Test Gini_Valid
0 Model 1 Model z domyślnymi parametrami i z zastosowaniem Label Encoding 16 97.05% 87.49% 86.22%
1 Model 2 Model z domyślnymi parametrami z zastosowaniem Dummy Encoding 42 96.68% 87.64% 85.63%

Alternatywny wariant modelu, wytrenowany na zmiennych typu Dummy, również wykazuje silne tendencje do przeuczenia. Warto zauważyć, że wartość współczynnika Gini na zbiorze treningowym okazała się niższa niż w przypadku pierwszego modelu. Zatem kodowanie binarne przełożyło się na minimalną poprawę wyników - na zbiorach testowym i walidacyjnym są nieznacznie wyższe, jednak zysk jakościowy jest na tyle mały, że różnica między oboma podejściami pozostaje marginalna.

Dodatkowo analiza ważności cech (zarówno oparta na zysku, jak i na permutacji) ujawniła analogiczne anomalie jak w modelu bazowym, potwierdzając obecność szumu.

W związku z tym, że sama manipulacja sposobem kodowania danych nie rozwiązała problemu nadmiernego dopasowania, w kolejnym kroku wdrożono optymalizację bayesowską. Celem tego zabiegu jest weryfikacja, czy ta optymalizacja pozwoli na redukcję overfittingu oraz poprawę stabilności predykcji.

1.2 Modele z parametrami wybranych poprzez optymalizację Bayesa¶

In [30]:
model3 = xgb.XGBClassifier()

params_space = {
    'n_estimators': Integer(50, 200),
    'max_depth': Integer(3, 10),
    'min_child_weight': Integer(1, 200),
    'learning_rate': Real(0.01, 0.3, prior='log-uniform'),
    'subsample': Real(0.6, 1.0),
    'colsample_bytree': Real(0.6, 1.0),
    'gamma': Real(1e-4, 10, prior='log-uniform'),
    'reg_lambda': Real(1e-4, 10, prior='log-uniform'),
    'reg_alpha': Real(1e-4, 10, prior='log-uniform'),
}

grid_seach1 = BayesSearchCV(
    estimator=model3, 
    search_spaces=params_space,
    scoring='neg_mean_squared_error',
    n_iter=50,
    cv=3,
    verbose=0,
    random_state=42
)

grid_seach1.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[30]:
BayesSearchCV(cv=3,
              estimator=XGBClassifier(base_score=None, booster=None,
                                      callbacks=None, colsample_bylevel=None,
                                      colsample_bynode=None,
                                      colsample_bytree=None, device=None,
                                      early_stopping_rounds=None,
                                      enable_categorical=False,
                                      eval_metric=None, feature_types=None,
                                      feature_weights=None, gamma=None,
                                      grow_policy=None, importance_type=None,
                                      interaction_constraints=No...
                             'min_child_weight': Integer(low=1, high=200, prior='uniform', transform='normalize'),
                             'n_estimators': Integer(low=50, high=200, prior='uniform', transform='normalize'),
                             'reg_alpha': Real(low=0.0001, high=10, prior='log-uniform', transform='normalize'),
                             'reg_lambda': Real(low=0.0001, high=10, prior='log-uniform', transform='normalize'),
                             'subsample': Real(low=0.6, high=1.0, prior='uniform', transform='normalize')})

Parameters
estimator XGBClassifier...ree=None, ...)
search_spaces {'colsample_bytree': Real(low=0.6,...m='normalize'), 'gamma': Real(low=0.00...m='normalize'), 'learning_rate': Real(low=0.01...m='normalize'), 'max_depth': Integer(low=3...m='normalize'), ...}
optimizer_kwargs None
n_iter 50
scoring 'neg_mean_squared_error'
fit_params None
n_jobs 1
n_points 1
iid 'deprecated'
refit True
cv 3
verbose 0
pre_dispatch '2*n_jobs'
random_state 42
error_score 'raise'
return_train_score False 
XGBClassifier(base_score=None, booster=None, callbacks=None,
              colsample_bylevel=None, colsample_bynode=None,
              colsample_bytree=1.0, device=None, early_stopping_rounds=None,
              enable_categorical=False, eval_metric=None, feature_types=None,
              feature_weights=None, gamma=0.0001, grow_policy=None,
              importance_type=None, interaction_constraints=None,
              learning_rate=0.0355899249071341, max_bin=None,
              max_cat_threshold=None, max_cat_to_onehot=None,
              max_delta_step=None, max_depth=10, max_leaves=None,
              min_child_weight=1, missing=nan, monotone_constraints=None,
              multi_strategy=None, n_estimators=200, n_jobs=None,
              num_parallel_tree=None, ...)
Parameters
objective objective: typing.Union[str, xgboost.sklearn._SklObjWProto, typing.Callable[[typing.Any, typing.Any], typing.Tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]], NoneType]

Specify the learning task and the corresponding learning objective or a custom
objective function to be used.

For custom objective, see :doc:`/tutorials/custom_metric_obj` and
:ref:`custom-obj-metric` for more information, along with the end note for
function signatures. 		'binary:logistic'
base_score base_score: typing.Union[float, typing.List[float], NoneType]

The initial prediction score of all instances, global bias. 		None
booster None
callbacks callbacks: typing.Optional[typing.List[xgboost.callback.TrainingCallback]]

List of callback functions that are applied at end of each iteration.
It is possible to use predefined callbacks by using
:ref:`Callback API `.

.. note::

States in callback are not preserved during training, which means callback
objects can not be reused for multiple training sessions without
reinitialization or deepcopy.

.. code-block:: python

for params in parameters_grid:
# be sure to (re)initialize the callbacks before each run
callbacks = [xgb.callback.LearningRateScheduler(custom_rates)]
reg = xgboost.XGBRegressor(**params, callbacks=callbacks)
reg.fit(X, y) 		None
colsample_bylevel colsample_bylevel: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns for each level. 		None
colsample_bynode colsample_bynode: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns for each split. 		None
colsample_bytree colsample_bytree: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns when constructing each tree. 		1.0
device device: typing.Optional[str]

.. versionadded:: 2.0.0

Device ordinal, available options are `cpu`, `cuda`, and `gpu`. 		None
early_stopping_rounds early_stopping_rounds: typing.Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

- Activates early stopping. Validation metric needs to improve at least once in
every **early_stopping_rounds** round(s) to continue training. Requires at
least one item in **eval_set** in :py:meth:`fit`.

- If early stopping occurs, the model will have two additional attributes:
:py:attr:`best_score` and :py:attr:`best_iteration`. These are used by the
:py:meth:`predict` and :py:meth:`apply` methods to determine the optimal
number of trees during inference. If users want to access the full model
(including trees built after early stopping), they can specify the
`iteration_range` in these inference methods. In addition, other utilities
like model plotting can also use the entire model.

- If you prefer to discard the trees after `best_iteration`, consider using the
callback function :py:class:`xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping`.

- If there's more than one item in **eval_set**, the last entry will be used for
early stopping. If there's more than one metric in **eval_metric**, the last
metric will be used for early stopping. 		None
enable_categorical enable_categorical: bool

See the same parameter of :py:class:`DMatrix` for details. 		False
eval_metric eval_metric: typing.Union[str, typing.List[typing.Union[str, typing.Callable]], typing.Callable, NoneType]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

Metric used for monitoring the training result and early stopping. It can be a
string or list of strings as names of predefined metric in XGBoost (See
:doc:`/parameter`), one of the metrics in :py:mod:`sklearn.metrics`, or any
other user defined metric that looks like `sklearn.metrics`.

If custom objective is also provided, then custom metric should implement the
corresponding reverse link function.

Unlike the `scoring` parameter commonly used in scikit-learn, when a callable
object is provided, it's assumed to be a cost function and by default XGBoost
will minimize the result during early stopping.

For advanced usage on Early stopping like directly choosing to maximize instead
of minimize, see :py:obj:`xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping`.

See :doc:`/tutorials/custom_metric_obj` and :ref:`custom-obj-metric` for more
information.

.. code-block:: python

from sklearn.datasets import load_diabetes
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
X, y = load_diabetes(return_X_y=True)
reg = xgb.XGBRegressor(
tree_method="hist",
eval_metric=mean_absolute_error,
)
reg.fit(X, y, eval_set=[(X, y)]) 		None
feature_types feature_types: typing.Optional[typing.Sequence[str]]

.. versionadded:: 1.7.0

Used for specifying feature types without constructing a dataframe. See
the :py:class:`DMatrix` for details. 		None
feature_weights feature_weights: Optional[ArrayLike]

Weight for each feature, defines the probability of each feature being selected
when colsample is being used. All values must be greater than 0, otherwise a
`ValueError` is thrown. 		None
gamma gamma: typing.Optional[float]

(min_split_loss) Minimum loss reduction required to make a further partition on
a leaf node of the tree. 		0.0001
grow_policy grow_policy: typing.Optional[str]

Tree growing policy.

- depthwise: Favors splitting at nodes closest to the node,
- lossguide: Favors splitting at nodes with highest loss change. 		None
importance_type None
interaction_constraints interaction_constraints: typing.Union[str, typing.List[typing.Tuple[str]], NoneType]

Constraints for interaction representing permitted interactions. The
constraints must be specified in the form of a nested list, e.g. ``[[0, 1], [2,
3, 4]]``, where each inner list is a group of indices of features that are
allowed to interact with each other. See :doc:`tutorial
` for more information 		None
learning_rate learning_rate: typing.Optional[float]

Boosting learning rate (xgb's "eta") 		0.0355899249071341
max_bin max_bin: typing.Optional[int]

If using histogram-based algorithm, maximum number of bins per feature 		None
max_cat_threshold max_cat_threshold: typing.Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.7.0

.. note:: This parameter is experimental

Maximum number of categories considered for each split. Used only by
partition-based splits for preventing over-fitting. Also, `enable_categorical`
needs to be set to have categorical feature support. See :doc:`Categorical Data
` and :ref:`cat-param` for details. 		None
max_cat_to_onehot max_cat_to_onehot: Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

.. note:: This parameter is experimental

A threshold for deciding whether XGBoost should use one-hot encoding based split
for categorical data. When number of categories is lesser than the threshold
then one-hot encoding is chosen, otherwise the categories will be partitioned
into children nodes. Also, `enable_categorical` needs to be set to have
categorical feature support. See :doc:`Categorical Data
` and :ref:`cat-param` for details. 		None
max_delta_step max_delta_step: typing.Optional[float]

Maximum delta step we allow each tree's weight estimation to be. 		None
max_depth max_depth: typing.Optional[int]

Maximum tree depth for base learners. 		10
max_leaves max_leaves: typing.Optional[int]

Maximum number of leaves; 0 indicates no limit. 		None
min_child_weight min_child_weight: typing.Optional[float]

Minimum sum of instance weight(hessian) needed in a child. 		1
missing missing: float

Value in the data which needs to be present as a missing value. Default to
:py:data:`numpy.nan`. 		nan
monotone_constraints monotone_constraints: typing.Union[typing.Dict[str, int], str, NoneType]

Constraint of variable monotonicity. See :doc:`tutorial `
for more information. 		None
multi_strategy multi_strategy: typing.Optional[str]

.. versionadded:: 2.0.0

.. note:: This parameter is working-in-progress.

The strategy used for training multi-target models, including multi-target
regression and multi-class classification. See :doc:`/tutorials/multioutput` for
more information.

- ``one_output_per_tree``: One model for each target.
- ``multi_output_tree``: Use multi-target trees. 		None
n_estimators n_estimators: Optional[int]

Number of boosting rounds. 		200
n_jobs n_jobs: typing.Optional[int]

Number of parallel threads used to run xgboost. When used with other
Scikit-Learn algorithms like grid search, you may choose which algorithm to
parallelize and balance the threads. Creating thread contention will
significantly slow down both algorithms. 		None
num_parallel_tree None
random_state random_state: typing.Union[numpy.random.mtrand.RandomState, numpy.random._generator.Generator, int, NoneType]

Random number seed.

.. note::

Using gblinear booster with shotgun updater is nondeterministic as
it uses Hogwild algorithm. 		None
reg_alpha reg_alpha: typing.Optional[float]

L1 regularization term on weights (xgb's alpha). 		0.0001
reg_lambda reg_lambda: typing.Optional[float]

L2 regularization term on weights (xgb's lambda). 		10.0
sampling_method sampling_method: typing.Optional[str]

Sampling method. Used only by the GPU version of ``hist`` tree method.

- ``uniform``: Select random training instances uniformly.
- ``gradient_based``: Select random training instances with higher probability
when the gradient and hessian are larger. (cf. CatBoost) 		None
scale_pos_weight scale_pos_weight: typing.Optional[float]

Balancing of positive and negative weights. 		None
subsample subsample: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of the training instance. 		0.9511151124001329
tree_method tree_method: typing.Optional[str]

Specify which tree method to use. Default to auto. If this parameter is set to
default, XGBoost will choose the most conservative option available. It's
recommended to study this option from the parameters document :doc:`tree method
` 		None
validate_parameters validate_parameters: typing.Optional[bool]

Give warnings for unknown parameter. 		None
verbosity verbosity: typing.Optional[int]

The degree of verbosity. Valid values are 0 (silent) - 3 (debug). 		None
In [ ]:
grid_seach1.best_params_
Out[ ]:
OrderedDict([('colsample_bytree', 1.0),
             ('gamma', 0.42210016413900475),
             ('learning_rate', 0.04078026871684014),
             ('max_depth', 6),
             ('min_child_weight', 1),
             ('n_estimators', 200),
             ('reg_alpha', 0.07037434451736549),
             ('reg_lambda', 0.20287221997398708),
             ('subsample', 0.7601836719302151)])
In [ ]:
get_feats_plots(grid_seach1.best_estimator_, X_train, y_train)
In [ ]:
get_feats_plots(grid_seach1.best_estimator_, X_test, y_test)
In [ ]:
model3_results = model_summary(grid_seach1.best_estimator_, 'Model 3', 'Model z parametrami wybranymi w optymalizacji Bayesa z zastosowaniem Label Encoding',
                                X_train, X_test, X_valid, 
                                y_train, y_test, y_valid)

models_results = pd.concat([models_results, model3_results]).reset_index(drop = True)
models_results.style.format('{:.2%}', subset = ['Gini_Train', 'Gini_Test', 'Gini_Valid'])
Out[ ]:
  Model Model_Description Number_of_Vars Gini_Train Gini_Test Gini_Valid
0 Model 1 Model z domyślnymi parametrami i z zastosowaniem Label Encoding 16 97.05% 87.49% 86.22%
1 Model 2 Model z domyślnymi parametrami z zastosowaniem Dummy Encoding 42 96.68% 87.64% 85.63%
2 Model 3 Model z parametrami wybranymi w optymalizacji Bayesa z zastosowaniem Label Encoding 16 92.70% 88.26% 87.14%

Zastosowanie optymalizacji bayesowskiej dla modelu opartego na zmiennych zakodowanych numerycznie (Label Encoding) przyniosło oczekiwaną poprawę wyników. W rezultacie otrzymaliśmy kontrolowany spadek współczynnika Gini na zbiorze treningowym przy jednoczesnym wzroście tej metryki na danych testowych i walidacyjnych. Zabieg ten skutecznie zredukował zjawisko przeuczenia.

Kluczowym dowodem na poprawę struktury modelu jest stabilizacja ważności cech. Ewaluacja ważności permutacyjnej na zbiorach testowym oraz walidacyjnym wykazała, że po optymalizacji żadna z uwzględnionych zmiennych nie generuje szumu i nie wpływa negatywnie na globalną moc dyskryminacyjną klasyfikatora.

Mając na uwadze sukces optymalizacji parametrycznej, następnie zweryfikujemy czy znalezione hiperparametry przeniosą się na alternatywną przestrzeń cech, w tym przypadku na zmienne typu Dummy.

In [ ]:
model4 = xgb.XGBClassifier(**grid_seach1.best_params_)
model4.fit(X_train_d, y_train)
Out[ ]:
XGBClassifier(base_score=None, booster=None, callbacks=None,
              colsample_bylevel=None, colsample_bynode=None,
              colsample_bytree=1.0, device=None, early_stopping_rounds=None,
              enable_categorical=False, eval_metric=None, feature_types=None,
              feature_weights=None, gamma=0.42210016413900475, grow_policy=None,
              importance_type=None, interaction_constraints=None,
              learning_rate=0.04078026871684014, max_bin=None,
              max_cat_threshold=None, max_cat_to_onehot=None,
              max_delta_step=None, max_depth=6, max_leaves=None,
              min_child_weight=1, missing=nan, monotone_constraints=None,
              multi_strategy=None, n_estimators=200, n_jobs=None,
              num_parallel_tree=None, ...)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
objective objective: typing.Union[str, xgboost.sklearn._SklObjWProto, typing.Callable[[typing.Any, typing.Any], typing.Tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]], NoneType]

Specify the learning task and the corresponding learning objective or a custom
objective function to be used.

For custom objective, see :doc:`/tutorials/custom_metric_obj` and
:ref:`custom-obj-metric` for more information, along with the end note for
function signatures. 		'binary:logistic'
base_score base_score: typing.Union[float, typing.List[float], NoneType]

The initial prediction score of all instances, global bias. 		None
booster None
callbacks callbacks: typing.Optional[typing.List[xgboost.callback.TrainingCallback]]

List of callback functions that are applied at end of each iteration.
It is possible to use predefined callbacks by using
:ref:`Callback API `.

.. note::

States in callback are not preserved during training, which means callback
objects can not be reused for multiple training sessions without
reinitialization or deepcopy.

.. code-block:: python

for params in parameters_grid:
# be sure to (re)initialize the callbacks before each run
callbacks = [xgb.callback.LearningRateScheduler(custom_rates)]
reg = xgboost.XGBRegressor(**params, callbacks=callbacks)
reg.fit(X, y) 		None
colsample_bylevel colsample_bylevel: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns for each level. 		None
colsample_bynode colsample_bynode: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns for each split. 		None
colsample_bytree colsample_bytree: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns when constructing each tree. 		1.0
device device: typing.Optional[str]

.. versionadded:: 2.0.0

Device ordinal, available options are `cpu`, `cuda`, and `gpu`. 		None
early_stopping_rounds early_stopping_rounds: typing.Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

- Activates early stopping. Validation metric needs to improve at least once in
every **early_stopping_rounds** round(s) to continue training. Requires at
least one item in **eval_set** in :py:meth:`fit`.

- If early stopping occurs, the model will have two additional attributes:
:py:attr:`best_score` and :py:attr:`best_iteration`. These are used by the
:py:meth:`predict` and :py:meth:`apply` methods to determine the optimal
number of trees during inference. If users want to access the full model
(including trees built after early stopping), they can specify the
`iteration_range` in these inference methods. In addition, other utilities
like model plotting can also use the entire model.

- If you prefer to discard the trees after `best_iteration`, consider using the
callback function :py:class:`xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping`.

- If there's more than one item in **eval_set**, the last entry will be used for
early stopping. If there's more than one metric in **eval_metric**, the last
metric will be used for early stopping. 		None
enable_categorical enable_categorical: bool

See the same parameter of :py:class:`DMatrix` for details. 		False
eval_metric eval_metric: typing.Union[str, typing.List[typing.Union[str, typing.Callable]], typing.Callable, NoneType]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

Metric used for monitoring the training result and early stopping. It can be a
string or list of strings as names of predefined metric in XGBoost (See
:doc:`/parameter`), one of the metrics in :py:mod:`sklearn.metrics`, or any
other user defined metric that looks like `sklearn.metrics`.

If custom objective is also provided, then custom metric should implement the
corresponding reverse link function.

Unlike the `scoring` parameter commonly used in scikit-learn, when a callable
object is provided, it's assumed to be a cost function and by default XGBoost
will minimize the result during early stopping.

For advanced usage on Early stopping like directly choosing to maximize instead
of minimize, see :py:obj:`xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping`.

See :doc:`/tutorials/custom_metric_obj` and :ref:`custom-obj-metric` for more
information.

.. code-block:: python

from sklearn.datasets import load_diabetes
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
X, y = load_diabetes(return_X_y=True)
reg = xgb.XGBRegressor(
tree_method="hist",
eval_metric=mean_absolute_error,
)
reg.fit(X, y, eval_set=[(X, y)]) 		None
feature_types feature_types: typing.Optional[typing.Sequence[str]]

.. versionadded:: 1.7.0

Used for specifying feature types without constructing a dataframe. See
the :py:class:`DMatrix` for details. 		None
feature_weights feature_weights: Optional[ArrayLike]

Weight for each feature, defines the probability of each feature being selected
when colsample is being used. All values must be greater than 0, otherwise a
`ValueError` is thrown. 		None
gamma gamma: typing.Optional[float]

(min_split_loss) Minimum loss reduction required to make a further partition on
a leaf node of the tree. 		0.42210016413900475
grow_policy grow_policy: typing.Optional[str]

Tree growing policy.

- depthwise: Favors splitting at nodes closest to the node,
- lossguide: Favors splitting at nodes with highest loss change. 		None
importance_type None
interaction_constraints interaction_constraints: typing.Union[str, typing.List[typing.Tuple[str]], NoneType]

Constraints for interaction representing permitted interactions. The
constraints must be specified in the form of a nested list, e.g. ``[[0, 1], [2,
3, 4]]``, where each inner list is a group of indices of features that are
allowed to interact with each other. See :doc:`tutorial
` for more information 		None
learning_rate learning_rate: typing.Optional[float]

Boosting learning rate (xgb's "eta") 		0.04078026871684014
max_bin max_bin: typing.Optional[int]

If using histogram-based algorithm, maximum number of bins per feature 		None
max_cat_threshold max_cat_threshold: typing.Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.7.0

.. note:: This parameter is experimental

Maximum number of categories considered for each split. Used only by
partition-based splits for preventing over-fitting. Also, `enable_categorical`
needs to be set to have categorical feature support. See :doc:`Categorical Data
` and :ref:`cat-param` for details. 		None
max_cat_to_onehot max_cat_to_onehot: Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

.. note:: This parameter is experimental

A threshold for deciding whether XGBoost should use one-hot encoding based split
for categorical data. When number of categories is lesser than the threshold
then one-hot encoding is chosen, otherwise the categories will be partitioned
into children nodes. Also, `enable_categorical` needs to be set to have
categorical feature support. See :doc:`Categorical Data
` and :ref:`cat-param` for details. 		None
max_delta_step max_delta_step: typing.Optional[float]

Maximum delta step we allow each tree's weight estimation to be. 		None
max_depth max_depth: typing.Optional[int]

Maximum tree depth for base learners. 		6
max_leaves max_leaves: typing.Optional[int]

Maximum number of leaves; 0 indicates no limit. 		None
min_child_weight min_child_weight: typing.Optional[float]

Minimum sum of instance weight(hessian) needed in a child. 		1
missing missing: float

Value in the data which needs to be present as a missing value. Default to
:py:data:`numpy.nan`. 		nan
monotone_constraints monotone_constraints: typing.Union[typing.Dict[str, int], str, NoneType]

Constraint of variable monotonicity. See :doc:`tutorial `
for more information. 		None
multi_strategy multi_strategy: typing.Optional[str]

.. versionadded:: 2.0.0

.. note:: This parameter is working-in-progress.

The strategy used for training multi-target models, including multi-target
regression and multi-class classification. See :doc:`/tutorials/multioutput` for
more information.

- ``one_output_per_tree``: One model for each target.
- ``multi_output_tree``: Use multi-target trees. 		None
n_estimators n_estimators: Optional[int]

Number of boosting rounds. 		200
n_jobs n_jobs: typing.Optional[int]

Number of parallel threads used to run xgboost. When used with other
Scikit-Learn algorithms like grid search, you may choose which algorithm to
parallelize and balance the threads. Creating thread contention will
significantly slow down both algorithms. 		None
num_parallel_tree None
random_state random_state: typing.Union[numpy.random.mtrand.RandomState, numpy.random._generator.Generator, int, NoneType]

Random number seed.

.. note::

Using gblinear booster with shotgun updater is nondeterministic as
it uses Hogwild algorithm. 		None
reg_alpha reg_alpha: typing.Optional[float]

L1 regularization term on weights (xgb's alpha). 		0.07037434451736549
reg_lambda reg_lambda: typing.Optional[float]

L2 regularization term on weights (xgb's lambda). 		0.20287221997398708
sampling_method sampling_method: typing.Optional[str]

Sampling method. Used only by the GPU version of ``hist`` tree method.

- ``uniform``: Select random training instances uniformly.
- ``gradient_based``: Select random training instances with higher probability
when the gradient and hessian are larger. (cf. CatBoost) 		None
scale_pos_weight scale_pos_weight: typing.Optional[float]

Balancing of positive and negative weights. 		None
subsample subsample: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of the training instance. 		0.7601836719302151
tree_method tree_method: typing.Optional[str]

Specify which tree method to use. Default to auto. If this parameter is set to
default, XGBoost will choose the most conservative option available. It's
recommended to study this option from the parameters document :doc:`tree method
` 		None
validate_parameters validate_parameters: typing.Optional[bool]

Give warnings for unknown parameter. 		None
verbosity verbosity: typing.Optional[int]

The degree of verbosity. Valid values are 0 (silent) - 3 (debug). 		None
In [ ]:
get_feats_plots(model4, X_train_d, y_train)
In [ ]:
get_feats_plots(model4, X_test_d, y_test)
In [ ]:
model4_results =  model_summary(model4, 'Model 4', 
                                'Model z parametrami wybranymi w optymalizacji Bayesa dla Modelu 3 z zastosowaniem Dummy Encoding',
                                X_train_d, X_test_d, X_valid_d, 
                                y_train, y_test, y_valid)

models_results = pd.concat([models_results, model4_results]).reset_index(drop = True)
models_results.style.format('{:.2%}', subset = ['Gini_Train', 'Gini_Test', 'Gini_Valid'])
Out[ ]:
  Model Model_Description Number_of_Vars Gini_Train Gini_Test Gini_Valid
0 Model 1 Model z domyślnymi parametrami i z zastosowaniem Label Encoding 16 97.05% 87.49% 86.22%
1 Model 2 Model z domyślnymi parametrami z zastosowaniem Dummy Encoding 42 96.68% 87.64% 85.63%
2 Model 3 Model z parametrami wybranymi w optymalizacji Bayesa z zastosowaniem Label Encoding 16 92.70% 88.26% 87.14%
3 Model 4 Model z parametrami wybranymi w optymalizacji Bayesa dla Modelu 3 z zastosowaniem Dummy Encoding 42 92.18% 88.46% 87.24%

Zastosowanie hiperparametrów znalezionych w poprzednim kroku do przestrzeni zmiennych typu dummy przyniosła wyraźną poprawę w stosunku do wariantu domyślnego. Można zaobserwować redukcję nadmiernego dopasowania na zbiorze treningowym oraz wzrost współczynnika Gini na zbiorach niezależnych. Co więcej, wariant ten osiągnął nieznacznie wyższą moc dyskryminacyjną na danych testowych i walidacyjnych niż zoptymalizowany model oparty na kodowaniu numerycznym (Label Encoding).

Pomimo obiecujących wartości metryk globalnych, ważności cech wyznaczone metodami Gain oraz ważności permutacyjnej straciły swoją spójność pomiędzy zbiorem treningowym a testowym. Permutacja na danych testowych ponownie wykazała obecność szumu wokół niektórych zmiennych, co bezpośrednio obniża końcowy potencjał klasyfikatora.

Zaobsetwowana niespójność wynika z faktu, że przeniesienie hiperparametrów do znacznie szerszej macierzy zmiennych okazało się nieoptymalne – model budowany na innych zmiennych wymaga niezależnej optymalizacji. W związku z tym zastosujemy procedurę optymalizacji bezpośrednio na zbiorze ze zmiennymi typu Dummy.

In [ ]:
model5 = xgb.XGBClassifier()

grid_seach2 = BayesSearchCV(
    estimator=model5, 
    search_spaces=params_space,
    scoring='neg_mean_squared_error',
    n_iter=50,
    cv=3,
    verbose=0,
    random_state=42
)

grid_seach2.fit(X_train_d, y_train)
Out[ ]:
BayesSearchCV(cv=3,
              estimator=XGBClassifier(base_score=None, booster=None,
                                      callbacks=None, colsample_bylevel=None,
                                      colsample_bynode=None,
                                      colsample_bytree=None, device=None,
                                      early_stopping_rounds=None,
                                      enable_categorical=False,
                                      eval_metric=None, feature_types=None,
                                      feature_weights=None, gamma=None,
                                      grow_policy=None, importance_type=None,
                                      interaction_constraints=No...
                             'min_child_weight': Integer(low=1, high=200, prior='uniform', transform='normalize'),
                             'n_estimators': Integer(low=50, high=200, prior='uniform', transform='normalize'),
                             'reg_alpha': Real(low=0.0001, high=10, prior='log-uniform', transform='normalize'),
                             'reg_lambda': Real(low=0.0001, high=10, prior='log-uniform', transform='normalize'),
                             'subsample': Real(low=0.6, high=1.0, prior='uniform', transform='normalize')})

Parameters
estimator XGBClassifier...ree=None, ...)
search_spaces {'colsample_bytree': Real(low=0.6,...m='normalize'), 'gamma': Real(low=0.00...m='normalize'), 'learning_rate': Real(low=0.01...m='normalize'), 'max_depth': Integer(low=3...m='normalize'), ...}

n_iter 50
scoring 'neg_mean_squared_error'
fit_params None
n_jobs 1
n_points 1
iid 'deprecated'
refit True
cv 3
verbose 0
pre_dispatch '2*n_jobs'
random_state 42
error_score 'raise'
return_train_score False 
XGBClassifier(base_score=None, booster=None, callbacks=None,
              colsample_bylevel=None, colsample_bynode=None,
              colsample_bytree=0.8839152274490003, device=None,
              early_stopping_rounds=None, enable_categorical=False,
              eval_metric=None, feature_types=None, feature_weights=None,

              max_delta_step=None, max_depth=5, max_leaves=None,
              min_child_weight=8, missing=nan, monotone_constraints=None,
              multi_strategy=None, n_estimators=200, n_jobs=None,
              num_parallel_tree=None, ...)
Parameters
objective objective: typing.Union[str, xgboost.sklearn._SklObjWProto, typing.Callable[[typing.Any, typing.Any], typing.Tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]], NoneType]

Specify the learning task and the corresponding learning objective or a custom
objective function to be used.

For custom objective, see :doc:`/tutorials/custom_metric_obj` and
:ref:`custom-obj-metric` for more information, along with the end note for
function signatures. 		'binary:logistic'
base_score base_score: typing.Union[float, typing.List[float], NoneType]

The initial prediction score of all instances, global bias. 		None
booster None
callbacks callbacks: typing.Optional[typing.List[xgboost.callback.TrainingCallback]]

List of callback functions that are applied at end of each iteration.
It is possible to use predefined callbacks by using
:ref:`Callback API `.

.. note::

States in callback are not preserved during training, which means callback
objects can not be reused for multiple training sessions without
reinitialization or deepcopy.

.. code-block:: python

for params in parameters_grid:
# be sure to (re)initialize the callbacks before each run
callbacks = [xgb.callback.LearningRateScheduler(custom_rates)]
reg = xgboost.XGBRegressor(**params, callbacks=callbacks)
reg.fit(X, y) 		None
colsample_bylevel colsample_bylevel: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns for each level. 		None
colsample_bynode colsample_bynode: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns for each split. 		None
colsample_bytree colsample_bytree: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns when constructing each tree. 		0.8839152274490003
device device: typing.Optional[str]

.. versionadded:: 2.0.0

Device ordinal, available options are `cpu`, `cuda`, and `gpu`. 		None
early_stopping_rounds early_stopping_rounds: typing.Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

- Activates early stopping. Validation metric needs to improve at least once in
every **early_stopping_rounds** round(s) to continue training. Requires at
least one item in **eval_set** in :py:meth:`fit`.

- If early stopping occurs, the model will have two additional attributes:
:py:attr:`best_score` and :py:attr:`best_iteration`. These are used by the
:py:meth:`predict` and :py:meth:`apply` methods to determine the optimal
number of trees during inference. If users want to access the full model
(including trees built after early stopping), they can specify the
`iteration_range` in these inference methods. In addition, other utilities
like model plotting can also use the entire model.

- If you prefer to discard the trees after `best_iteration`, consider using the
callback function :py:class:`xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping`.

- If there's more than one item in **eval_set**, the last entry will be used for
early stopping. If there's more than one metric in **eval_metric**, the last
metric will be used for early stopping. 		None
enable_categorical enable_categorical: bool

See the same parameter of :py:class:`DMatrix` for details. 		False
eval_metric eval_metric: typing.Union[str, typing.List[typing.Union[str, typing.Callable]], typing.Callable, NoneType]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

Metric used for monitoring the training result and early stopping. It can be a
string or list of strings as names of predefined metric in XGBoost (See
:doc:`/parameter`), one of the metrics in :py:mod:`sklearn.metrics`, or any
other user defined metric that looks like `sklearn.metrics`.

If custom objective is also provided, then custom metric should implement the
corresponding reverse link function.

Unlike the `scoring` parameter commonly used in scikit-learn, when a callable
object is provided, it's assumed to be a cost function and by default XGBoost
will minimize the result during early stopping.

For advanced usage on Early stopping like directly choosing to maximize instead
of minimize, see :py:obj:`xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping`.

See :doc:`/tutorials/custom_metric_obj` and :ref:`custom-obj-metric` for more
information.

.. code-block:: python

from sklearn.datasets import load_diabetes
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
X, y = load_diabetes(return_X_y=True)
reg = xgb.XGBRegressor(
tree_method="hist",
eval_metric=mean_absolute_error,
)
reg.fit(X, y, eval_set=[(X, y)]) 		None
feature_types feature_types: typing.Optional[typing.Sequence[str]]

.. versionadded:: 1.7.0

Used for specifying feature types without constructing a dataframe. See
the :py:class:`DMatrix` for details. 		None
feature_weights feature_weights: Optional[ArrayLike]

Weight for each feature, defines the probability of each feature being selected
when colsample is being used. All values must be greater than 0, otherwise a
`ValueError` is thrown. 		None
gamma gamma: typing.Optional[float]

(min_split_loss) Minimum loss reduction required to make a further partition on
a leaf node of the tree. 		0.004176822964985784
grow_policy grow_policy: typing.Optional[str]

Tree growing policy.

- depthwise: Favors splitting at nodes closest to the node,
- lossguide: Favors splitting at nodes with highest loss change. 		None
importance_type None
interaction_constraints interaction_constraints: typing.Union[str, typing.List[typing.Tuple[str]], NoneType]

Constraints for interaction representing permitted interactions. The
constraints must be specified in the form of a nested list, e.g. ``[[0, 1], [2,
3, 4]]``, where each inner list is a group of indices of features that are
allowed to interact with each other. See :doc:`tutorial
` for more information 		None
learning_rate learning_rate: typing.Optional[float]

Boosting learning rate (xgb's "eta") 		0.051842218263107656
max_bin max_bin: typing.Optional[int]

If using histogram-based algorithm, maximum number of bins per feature 		None
max_cat_threshold max_cat_threshold: typing.Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.7.0

.. note:: This parameter is experimental

Maximum number of categories considered for each split. Used only by
partition-based splits for preventing over-fitting. Also, `enable_categorical`
needs to be set to have categorical feature support. See :doc:`Categorical Data
` and :ref:`cat-param` for details. 		None
max_cat_to_onehot max_cat_to_onehot: Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

.. note:: This parameter is experimental

A threshold for deciding whether XGBoost should use one-hot encoding based split
for categorical data. When number of categories is lesser than the threshold
then one-hot encoding is chosen, otherwise the categories will be partitioned
into children nodes. Also, `enable_categorical` needs to be set to have
categorical feature support. See :doc:`Categorical Data
` and :ref:`cat-param` for details. 		None
max_delta_step max_delta_step: typing.Optional[float]

Maximum delta step we allow each tree's weight estimation to be. 		None
max_depth max_depth: typing.Optional[int]

Maximum tree depth for base learners. 		5
max_leaves max_leaves: typing.Optional[int]

Maximum number of leaves; 0 indicates no limit. 		None
min_child_weight min_child_weight: typing.Optional[float]

Minimum sum of instance weight(hessian) needed in a child. 		8
missing missing: float

Value in the data which needs to be present as a missing value. Default to
:py:data:`numpy.nan`. 		nan
monotone_constraints monotone_constraints: typing.Union[typing.Dict[str, int], str, NoneType]

Constraint of variable monotonicity. See :doc:`tutorial `
for more information. 		None
multi_strategy multi_strategy: typing.Optional[str]

.. versionadded:: 2.0.0

.. note:: This parameter is working-in-progress.

The strategy used for training multi-target models, including multi-target
regression and multi-class classification. See :doc:`/tutorials/multioutput` for
more information.

- ``one_output_per_tree``: One model for each target.
- ``multi_output_tree``: Use multi-target trees. 		None
n_estimators n_estimators: Optional[int]

Number of boosting rounds. 		200
n_jobs n_jobs: typing.Optional[int]

Number of parallel threads used to run xgboost. When used with other
Scikit-Learn algorithms like grid search, you may choose which algorithm to
parallelize and balance the threads. Creating thread contention will
significantly slow down both algorithms. 		None
num_parallel_tree None
random_state random_state: typing.Union[numpy.random.mtrand.RandomState, numpy.random._generator.Generator, int, NoneType]

Random number seed.

.. note::

Using gblinear booster with shotgun updater is nondeterministic as
it uses Hogwild algorithm. 		None
reg_alpha reg_alpha: typing.Optional[float]

L1 regularization term on weights (xgb's alpha). 		0.9068357013924325
reg_lambda reg_lambda: typing.Optional[float]

L2 regularization term on weights (xgb's lambda). 		0.41698632372864874
sampling_method sampling_method: typing.Optional[str]

Sampling method. Used only by the GPU version of ``hist`` tree method.

- ``uniform``: Select random training instances uniformly.
- ``gradient_based``: Select random training instances with higher probability
when the gradient and hessian are larger. (cf. CatBoost) 		None
scale_pos_weight scale_pos_weight: typing.Optional[float]

Balancing of positive and negative weights. 		None
subsample subsample: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of the training instance. 		0.9921170354499472
tree_method tree_method: typing.Optional[str]

Specify which tree method to use. Default to auto. If this parameter is set to
default, XGBoost will choose the most conservative option available. It's
recommended to study this option from the parameters document :doc:`tree method
` 		None
validate_parameters validate_parameters: typing.Optional[bool]

Give warnings for unknown parameter. 		None
verbosity verbosity: typing.Optional[int]

The degree of verbosity. Valid values are 0 (silent) - 3 (debug). 		None
In [ ]:
grid_seach2.best_params_
Out[ ]:
OrderedDict([('colsample_bytree', 0.8839152274490003),
             ('gamma', 0.004176822964985784),
             ('learning_rate', 0.051842218263107656),
             ('max_depth', 5),
             ('min_child_weight', 8),
             ('n_estimators', 200),
             ('reg_alpha', 0.9068357013924325),
             ('reg_lambda', 0.41698632372864874),
             ('subsample', 0.9921170354499472)])
In [ ]:
get_feats_plots(grid_seach2.best_estimator_, X_train_d, y_train)
In [ ]:
get_feats_plots(grid_seach2.best_estimator_, X_test_d, y_test)

1.3 Finalne wyniki¶

In [ ]:
model5_results =  model_summary(grid_seach2.best_estimator_, 'Model 5',  
                                'Model z parametrami wybranymi w optymalizacji Bayesa z zastosowaniem Dummy Encoding',
                                X_train_d, X_test_d, X_valid_d, 
                                y_train, y_test, y_valid)

models_results = pd.concat([models_results, model5_results]).reset_index(drop = True)
models_results.style.format('{:.2%}', subset = ['Gini_Train', 'Gini_Test', 'Gini_Valid'])
Out[ ]:
  Model Model_Description Number_of_Vars Gini_Train Gini_Test Gini_Valid
0 Model 1 Model z domyślnymi parametrami i z zastosowaniem Label Encoding 16 97.05% 87.49% 86.22%
1 Model 2 Model z domyślnymi parametrami z zastosowaniem Dummy Encoding 42 96.68% 87.64% 85.63%
2 Model 3 Model z parametrami wybranymi w optymalizacji Bayesa z zastosowaniem Label Encoding 16 92.70% 88.26% 87.14%
3 Model 4 Model z parametrami wybranymi w optymalizacji Bayesa dla Modelu 3 z zastosowaniem Dummy Encoding 42 92.18% 88.46% 87.24%
4 Model 5 Model z parametrami wybranymi w optymalizacji Bayesa z zastosowaniem Dummy Encoding 42 89.05% 88.20% 86.85%

Dedykowana optymalizacja bayesowska przeprowadzona bezpośrednio na przestrzeni zmiennych typu dummy przyniosła dalszą poprawę jakości klasyfikatora. Wskaźnik Gini uległ ponownej poprawie, a zjawisko overfittingu zostało zredukowane do minimum, o czym świadczą zbliżone i stabilne wartości metryk na wszystkich trzech podzbiorach danych.

Natomiast w dalszym ciągu jest widoczny brak pełnej spójności w rankingach ważności cech pomiędzy zbiorem treningowym a testowym, sygnalizując obecność szumu. Warto podkreślić, że przy tak znacznym rozszerzeniu przestrzeni cech (wzrost do 42 zmiennych po zakodowaniu One-Hot) zjawisko to jest naturalną konsekwencją tzw. klątwy wymiarowości.

Mając na uwadze niejednoznaczne zachowanie metryk na pojedynczym podziale danych, kazdy wariant modelu XGBoost oddamy ostatecznej weryfikacji za pomocą procedury 10-krotnej walidacji krzyżowej (10-Fold Cross-Validation). Decyzja o wyborze 10 foldów jest podyktowana dużą liczebnością próby treningowej (27 126 obserwacji). Taki podział pozwoli nam na uzyskanie licznych (ok. 2700 rekordów) i reprezentatywnych podzbiorów walidacyjnych w każdej iteracji, co maksymalnie uwiarygadnia końcowe wnioski. Pozwoli to nam jednoznacznie rozstrzygnąć, czy zaobserwowane różnice oraz fluktuacje jakości predykcyjnej mają charakter statystycznie istotny, czy są jedynie dziełem przypadku wynikającym z losowego podziału prób.

In [7]:
# best_parameters_M3 = grid_seach1.best_params_
# best_parameters_M5 = grid_seach2.best_params_

# with open('best_params_M3_labeled.json', 'w') as f:
#     json.dump(best_parameters_M3, f, indent=4)

# with open('best_params_M5_dummy.json', 'w') as f:
#     json.dump(best_parameters_M5, f, indent=4)

with open('best_params_M3_labeled.json', 'r') as f:
    loaded_params_M3 = json.load(f)

with open('best_params_M5_dummy.json', 'r') as f:
    loaded_params_M5 = json.load(f)

model3_labeled = xgb.XGBClassifier(**loaded_params_M3)
model5_dummy = xgb.XGBClassifier(**loaded_params_M5)

model3_labeled.fit(X_train, y_train)
model5_dummy.fit(X_train_d, y_train)
Out[7]:
XGBClassifier(base_score=None, booster=None, callbacks=None,
              colsample_bylevel=None, colsample_bynode=None,
              colsample_bytree=0.8839152274490003, device=None,
              early_stopping_rounds=None, enable_categorical=False,
              eval_metric=None, feature_types=None, feature_weights=None,
              gamma=0.004176822964985784, grow_policy=None,
              importance_type=None, interaction_constraints=None,
              learning_rate=0.051842218263107656, max_bin=None,
              max_cat_threshold=None, max_cat_to_onehot=None,
              max_delta_step=None, max_depth=5, max_leaves=None,
              min_child_weight=8, missing=nan, monotone_constraints=None,
              multi_strategy=None, n_estimators=200, n_jobs=None,
              num_parallel_tree=None, ...)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
objective objective: typing.Union[str, xgboost.sklearn._SklObjWProto, typing.Callable[[typing.Any, typing.Any], typing.Tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]], NoneType]

Specify the learning task and the corresponding learning objective or a custom
objective function to be used.

For custom objective, see :doc:`/tutorials/custom_metric_obj` and
:ref:`custom-obj-metric` for more information, along with the end note for
function signatures. 		'binary:logistic'
base_score base_score: typing.Union[float, typing.List[float], NoneType]

The initial prediction score of all instances, global bias. 		None
booster None
callbacks callbacks: typing.Optional[typing.List[xgboost.callback.TrainingCallback]]

List of callback functions that are applied at end of each iteration.
It is possible to use predefined callbacks by using
:ref:`Callback API `.

.. note::

States in callback are not preserved during training, which means callback
objects can not be reused for multiple training sessions without
reinitialization or deepcopy.

.. code-block:: python

for params in parameters_grid:
# be sure to (re)initialize the callbacks before each run
callbacks = [xgb.callback.LearningRateScheduler(custom_rates)]
reg = xgboost.XGBRegressor(**params, callbacks=callbacks)
reg.fit(X, y) 		None
colsample_bylevel colsample_bylevel: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns for each level. 		None
colsample_bynode colsample_bynode: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns for each split. 		None
colsample_bytree colsample_bytree: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of columns when constructing each tree. 		0.8839152274490003
device device: typing.Optional[str]

.. versionadded:: 2.0.0

Device ordinal, available options are `cpu`, `cuda`, and `gpu`. 		None
early_stopping_rounds early_stopping_rounds: typing.Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

- Activates early stopping. Validation metric needs to improve at least once in
every **early_stopping_rounds** round(s) to continue training. Requires at
least one item in **eval_set** in :py:meth:`fit`.

- If early stopping occurs, the model will have two additional attributes:
:py:attr:`best_score` and :py:attr:`best_iteration`. These are used by the
:py:meth:`predict` and :py:meth:`apply` methods to determine the optimal
number of trees during inference. If users want to access the full model
(including trees built after early stopping), they can specify the
`iteration_range` in these inference methods. In addition, other utilities
like model plotting can also use the entire model.

- If you prefer to discard the trees after `best_iteration`, consider using the
callback function :py:class:`xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping`.

- If there's more than one item in **eval_set**, the last entry will be used for
early stopping. If there's more than one metric in **eval_metric**, the last
metric will be used for early stopping. 		None
enable_categorical enable_categorical: bool

See the same parameter of :py:class:`DMatrix` for details. 		False
eval_metric eval_metric: typing.Union[str, typing.List[typing.Union[str, typing.Callable]], typing.Callable, NoneType]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

Metric used for monitoring the training result and early stopping. It can be a
string or list of strings as names of predefined metric in XGBoost (See
:doc:`/parameter`), one of the metrics in :py:mod:`sklearn.metrics`, or any
other user defined metric that looks like `sklearn.metrics`.

If custom objective is also provided, then custom metric should implement the
corresponding reverse link function.

Unlike the `scoring` parameter commonly used in scikit-learn, when a callable
object is provided, it's assumed to be a cost function and by default XGBoost
will minimize the result during early stopping.

For advanced usage on Early stopping like directly choosing to maximize instead
of minimize, see :py:obj:`xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping`.

See :doc:`/tutorials/custom_metric_obj` and :ref:`custom-obj-metric` for more
information.

.. code-block:: python

from sklearn.datasets import load_diabetes
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
X, y = load_diabetes(return_X_y=True)
reg = xgb.XGBRegressor(
tree_method="hist",
eval_metric=mean_absolute_error,
)
reg.fit(X, y, eval_set=[(X, y)]) 		None
feature_types feature_types: typing.Optional[typing.Sequence[str]]

.. versionadded:: 1.7.0

Used for specifying feature types without constructing a dataframe. See
the :py:class:`DMatrix` for details. 		None
feature_weights feature_weights: Optional[ArrayLike]

Weight for each feature, defines the probability of each feature being selected
when colsample is being used. All values must be greater than 0, otherwise a
`ValueError` is thrown. 		None
gamma gamma: typing.Optional[float]

(min_split_loss) Minimum loss reduction required to make a further partition on
a leaf node of the tree. 		0.004176822964985784
grow_policy grow_policy: typing.Optional[str]

Tree growing policy.

- depthwise: Favors splitting at nodes closest to the node,
- lossguide: Favors splitting at nodes with highest loss change. 		None
importance_type None
interaction_constraints interaction_constraints: typing.Union[str, typing.List[typing.Tuple[str]], NoneType]

Constraints for interaction representing permitted interactions. The
constraints must be specified in the form of a nested list, e.g. ``[[0, 1], [2,
3, 4]]``, where each inner list is a group of indices of features that are
allowed to interact with each other. See :doc:`tutorial
` for more information 		None
learning_rate learning_rate: typing.Optional[float]

Boosting learning rate (xgb's "eta") 		0.051842218263107656
max_bin max_bin: typing.Optional[int]

If using histogram-based algorithm, maximum number of bins per feature 		None
max_cat_threshold max_cat_threshold: typing.Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.7.0

.. note:: This parameter is experimental

Maximum number of categories considered for each split. Used only by
partition-based splits for preventing over-fitting. Also, `enable_categorical`
needs to be set to have categorical feature support. See :doc:`Categorical Data
` and :ref:`cat-param` for details. 		None
max_cat_to_onehot max_cat_to_onehot: Optional[int]

.. versionadded:: 1.6.0

.. note:: This parameter is experimental

A threshold for deciding whether XGBoost should use one-hot encoding based split
for categorical data. When number of categories is lesser than the threshold
then one-hot encoding is chosen, otherwise the categories will be partitioned
into children nodes. Also, `enable_categorical` needs to be set to have
categorical feature support. See :doc:`Categorical Data
` and :ref:`cat-param` for details. 		None
max_delta_step max_delta_step: typing.Optional[float]

Maximum delta step we allow each tree's weight estimation to be. 		None
max_depth max_depth: typing.Optional[int]

Maximum tree depth for base learners. 		5
max_leaves max_leaves: typing.Optional[int]

Maximum number of leaves; 0 indicates no limit. 		None
min_child_weight min_child_weight: typing.Optional[float]

Minimum sum of instance weight(hessian) needed in a child. 		8
missing missing: float

Value in the data which needs to be present as a missing value. Default to
:py:data:`numpy.nan`. 		nan
monotone_constraints monotone_constraints: typing.Union[typing.Dict[str, int], str, NoneType]

Constraint of variable monotonicity. See :doc:`tutorial `
for more information. 		None
multi_strategy multi_strategy: typing.Optional[str]

.. versionadded:: 2.0.0

.. note:: This parameter is working-in-progress.

The strategy used for training multi-target models, including multi-target
regression and multi-class classification. See :doc:`/tutorials/multioutput` for
more information.

- ``one_output_per_tree``: One model for each target.
- ``multi_output_tree``: Use multi-target trees. 		None
n_estimators n_estimators: Optional[int]

Number of boosting rounds. 		200
n_jobs n_jobs: typing.Optional[int]

Number of parallel threads used to run xgboost. When used with other
Scikit-Learn algorithms like grid search, you may choose which algorithm to
parallelize and balance the threads. Creating thread contention will
significantly slow down both algorithms. 		None
num_parallel_tree None
random_state random_state: typing.Union[numpy.random.mtrand.RandomState, numpy.random._generator.Generator, int, NoneType]

Random number seed.

.. note::

Using gblinear booster with shotgun updater is nondeterministic as
it uses Hogwild algorithm. 		None
reg_alpha reg_alpha: typing.Optional[float]

L1 regularization term on weights (xgb's alpha). 		0.9068357013924325
reg_lambda reg_lambda: typing.Optional[float]

L2 regularization term on weights (xgb's lambda). 		0.41698632372864874
sampling_method sampling_method: typing.Optional[str]

Sampling method. Used only by the GPU version of ``hist`` tree method.

- ``uniform``: Select random training instances uniformly.
- ``gradient_based``: Select random training instances with higher probability
when the gradient and hessian are larger. (cf. CatBoost) 		None
scale_pos_weight scale_pos_weight: typing.Optional[float]

Balancing of positive and negative weights. 		None
subsample subsample: typing.Optional[float]

Subsample ratio of the training instance. 		0.9921170354499472
tree_method tree_method: typing.Optional[str]

Specify which tree method to use. Default to auto. If this parameter is set to
default, XGBoost will choose the most conservative option available. It's
recommended to study this option from the parameters document :doc:`tree method
` 		None
validate_parameters validate_parameters: typing.Optional[bool]

Give warnings for unknown parameter. 		None
verbosity verbosity: typing.Optional[int]

The degree of verbosity. Valid values are 0 (silent) - 3 (debug). 		None
In [28]:
gini_scorer = make_scorer(gini, response_method="predict_proba")

# równomierne rozłożenie y=1 po foldach
cv_strategy = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=10, shuffle=True, random_state=20)

model_1_xgb = xgb.XGBClassifier(**model1.get_params())
model_2_xgb = xgb.XGBClassifier(**model2.get_params())
model_3_xgb = xgb.XGBClassifier(**loaded_params_M3)
model_4_xgb = xgb.XGBClassifier(**loaded_params_M3)
model_5_xgb = xgb.XGBClassifier(**loaded_params_M5)

scores_m1 = cross_val_score(
    estimator=model_1_xgb, 
    X=X_train, 
    y=y_train, 
    cv=cv_strategy, 
    scoring=gini_scorer,
    n_jobs=2
)

scores_m2 = cross_val_score(
    estimator=model_2_xgb, 
    X=X_train_d, 
    y=y_train, 
    cv=cv_strategy, 
    scoring=gini_scorer,
    n_jobs=2
)

scores_m3 = cross_val_score(
    estimator=model_3_xgb, 
    X=X_train, 
    y=y_train, 
    cv=cv_strategy, 
    scoring=gini_scorer,
    n_jobs=2
)

scores_m4 = cross_val_score(
    estimator=model_4_xgb, 
    X=X_train_d, 
    y=y_train, 
    cv=cv_strategy, 
    scoring=gini_scorer,
    n_jobs=2
)

scores_m5= cross_val_score(
    estimator=model_5_xgb, 
    X=X_train_d, 
    y=y_train, 
    cv=cv_strategy, 
    scoring=gini_scorer,
    n_jobs=2
)

print("Współczynnik Gini z 10-krotnej walidacji krzyżowej:")
print(f"Model 1 (Label Encoding Bez Optymalizacji): Średni Gini = {np.mean(scores_m1):.2%} (+/- odchylenie std: {np.std(scores_m1):.2%})")
print(f"Model 2 (Dummy Encoding Bez Optymalizacji): Średni Gini = {np.mean(scores_m2):.2%} (+/- odchylenie std: {np.std(scores_m2):.2%})")
print(f"Model 3 (Label Encoding Z Optymalizacją): Średni Gini = {np.mean(scores_m3):.2%} (+/- odchylenie std: {np.std(scores_m3):.2%})")
print(f"Model 4 (Dummy Encoding Z Optymalizacją Z Modelu 3): Średni Gini = {np.mean(scores_m4):.2%} (+/- odchylenie std: {np.std(scores_m4):.2%})")
print(f"Model 5 (Dummy Encoding Z Optymalizacją ): Średni Gini = {np.mean(scores_m5):.2%} (+/- odchylenie std: {np.std(scores_m5):.2%})")

Współczynnik Gini z 10-krotnej walidacji krzyżowej:
Model 1 (Label Encoding Bez Optymalizacji): Średni Gini = 84.46% (+/- odchylenie std: 0.90%)
Model 2 (Dummy Encoding Bez Optymalizacji): Średni Gini = 84.48% (+/- odchylenie std: 0.79%)
Model 3 (Label Encoding Z Optymalizacją): Średni Gini = 85.83% (+/- odchylenie std: 0.94%)
Model 4 (Dummy Encoding Z Optymalizacją Z Modelu 3): Średni Gini = 85.93% (+/- odchylenie std: 0.75%)
Model 5 (Dummy Encoding Z Optymalizacją ): Średni Gini = 85.75% (+/- odchylenie std: 0.81%)

Zbiorcze zestawienie wyników 10-krotnej walidacji krzyżowej dla modeli pełnowymiarowych jednoznacznie wskazuje, że kluczowym czynnikiem podnoszącym jakość klasyfikacji była optymalizacja hiperparametrów za pomocą BayesSearchCV, generując stabilny przyrost średniego współczynnika Gini o około 1.4 p.p. w stosunku do modeli bazowych.

Najwyższą wartością współczynnika Gini na pełnej przestrzeni cech wykazał się wariant hybrydowy (Model 4), osiągając wynik na poziomie 85,93%. Ponieważ różnica efektywności między zoptymalizowanymi modelami opartymi na kodowaniu rzadkim (Model 4 i Model 5) okazała się statystycznie nieistotna i wyniosła zaledwie 0,18 p.p., do dalszego etapu redukcji wymiarowości skierowano Model 5, ponieważ algorytm optymalizacji Bayesowskiej poszukiwał dedykowanej konfiguracji hiperparametrów bezpośrednio na przestrzeni zmiennych fikcyjnych (Dummy Encoding), co zapewnia spójność strukturalną modelu z przetwarzanym zbiorem zmiennych danych.

Oprócz Modelu 5 przeanalizujemy Model 3, by zweryfikować która przestrzeń cech pozwoli na uzyskanie najwyższej i najbardziej stabilnej jakości predykcji.

Podsumowując etap testów wariantów bazowych: dwie najbardziej obiecujące architektury to zoptymalizowany model oparty na kodowaniu numerycznym (Model 3) oraz zoptymalizowany model oparty na zmiennych dummy (Model 5). W celu wyłonienia ostatecznego modelu champion, w kolejnym rozdziale spróbujemy zredukować wymiarowość w obu tych wariantach.

2. Wyłonienie modelu Champion¶

W celu wyłonienia modelu wiodącego (Championa), w obu najlepszych podstawowych wariantach modelu przeprowadzimy procedurę redukcji wymiarowości zmiennych objaśniających. Zastosowana zostanie sekwencyjna metoda Forward Selection, oparta na wyjściowym rankingu ważności predyktorów.

Proces ten będzie przebiegał według następującego algorytmu:

  1. W pierwszej kolejności budujemy model oparty wyłącznie na jednej, najwyżej ocenionej w rankingu cechy, a następnie wyznaczana jest jego zdolność dyskryminacyjna (Gini)

  2. W każdym kolejnym kroku do modelu dodajemy następną pod względem istotności zmienną z rankingu, po czym następuje ponowna ewaluacja jakości.

  3. Moment, w którym przyrost wartości współczynnika Gini staje się marginalny, wyznaczamy optymalne odcięcie i liczbę zmiennych w modelu.

Najpierw algorytm zastosowano do Modelu 3.

In [20]:
gini_var_results_M3_df = draw_gini_vs_variable(model3_labeled, 
                                            X_train, y_train, X_test, y_test, 
                                            params=loaded_params_M3,
                                            title_name = "Porównanie wpływu zmiennych na wartość Giniego w zbiorze treningowym i testowym - Model 3")
gini_var_results_M3_df.style.format('{:.2%}', subset = ['importance', 'gini_train', 'gini_test'])
Out[20]:
  variable importance gini_train gini_test
0 duration 6.60% 62.99% 61.73%
1 month 2.58% 76.30% 74.50%
2 pdays 1.91% 84.02% 80.95%
3 contact 1.57% 86.34% 83.69%
4 day 1.38% 89.72% 87.03%
5 balance 1.08% 90.49% 86.77%
6 age 0.95% 91.43% 87.36%
7 poutcome 0.81% 91.86% 87.87%
8 housing 0.54% 92.06% 87.99%
9 campaign 0.45% 92.37% 88.15%
10 job 0.44% 92.38% 88.03%
11 education 0.31% 92.54% 88.16%
12 marital 0.26% 92.69% 88.23%
13 loan 0.21% 92.61% 88.23%
14 previous 0.16% 92.66% 88.35%
15 default 0.01% 92.70% 88.26%

Analizując przebieg krzywej jakości modelu, można zaobserwować, że w okolicach 10. zmiennej (uszeregowanej według rankingu ważności) współczynnik Gini wchodzi w fazę plateau. Dalsza rozbudowa przestrzeni cech o kolejne predyktory nie przynosi już istotnego przyrostu mocy dyskryminacyjnej klasyfikatora.

Zatem do zredukowanej wersji Modelu 3 zakwalifikowano ostatecznie 10 kluczowych zmiennych: duration, month, pdays, contact, day, balance, age, poutcome, housing oraz campaign.

In [21]:
best_cols_M3 = gini_var_results_M3_df.iloc[0:10].variable
In [ ]:
model_champion_cand_labeled = xgb.XGBClassifier()

grid_search4 = BayesSearchCV(
    estimator=model_champion_cand_labeled, 
    search_spaces=params_space,
    scoring='neg_mean_squared_error',
    n_iter=50,
    cv=3,
    verbose=0,
    random_state=42
)
grid_search4.fit(X_train.loc[:, best_cols_M3], y_train)

              estimator=XGBClassifier(base_score=None, booster=None,
                                      callbacks=None, colsample_bylevel=None,
                                      colsample_bynode=None,

                                      early_stopping_rounds=None,
                                      enable_categorical=False,

                             'min_child_weight': Integer(low=1, high=200, prior='uniform', transform='normalize'),
                             'n_estimators': Integer(low=50, high=200, prior='uniform', transform='normalize'),
